Would you take it, just somebody stick your head out the door and see if they've put food out there yet or not? They did? Thank you. Okay, we'll do one more. Principle seven, principle of frame of reference. This is a biggie because ironically with all of the media and all of the technology that exists, people's frame of reference, their knowledge base is shrinking. It's not expanding. The reason for that is that media is all fragmented. When I grew up, everybody came home and sat down in front of the evening news and you had three choices and you got the whole evening news, whether you liked it or not. So you got the main financial news of the day, you got the main sports news of the day, you got the main national news of the day, you got the main international news of the day, you got the main political news of the day. So almost regardless of your level of interest in those various things, you had some basic level of understanding. There's an election going on, who's running any election? You kind of knew. The world series is going on, who's playing in the world series? You kind of knew. Well, that's all gone and that's not how most people now get information. They get information only in the very narrow, little fragmented place where they have interest. So the vast majority of the people come home and they watch ESPN and they hardly anybody watches evening news, by the way. The only reason anybody knows Katie Cork replaced Dan Rather on CBS News is because it was talked about on entertainment tonight. I mean, otherwise nobody would have known. Some people sit and just watch ESPN. Conservative people who have conservative political bent, they tune in to one of the two cable networks that gives them conservative political news, which really is a news at all, it's viewpoint. The people of liberal bent, they tune in to where they can get only what interests them. Some people only watch the Food Network and that's all they watch. Magazines, say Time and Newsweek, are struggling for survival because broad based magazines go look at a newsstand. It's all specialization. So people's knowledge base is actually shrinking, not expanding. And their frame of reference is shorter and shorter and shorter and shorter. I heard Donnie Deutsch say, and I don't know in what context, but I jotted it down. He made the statement the other night that advertisers now need to understand that history consists of now. It's a great statement because people's historical references are shorter and shorter and shorter and shorter. Because we tend to think more about what it is that we're doing and deliberately collect information from a variety of sources. Our frame of reference is probably longer in duration than the people we are trying to influence and our base of knowledge is probably bigger and broader and more diverse than the people we are trying to influence. So the danger is using things for which they have no frame of reference. And so the Leno quote, Jay said, my job is much harder than Johnny's was because Johnny didn't have to explain his jokes. And if you listen to Leno carefully, and the next time you listen to a monologue, I encourage you to do so. Leno is very, very good at and careful at providing frame of reference for every joke. So he tells you who everybody is. He tells you what everything is. He doesn't assume you know that Peyton Manning is a football player or that Hillary Clinton is running for president. If he does a Hillary joke, he tells you who the hell Hillary is because he's learned. You can't assume. And if you tell him the joke that they don't have frame of reference for, you ain't going to get a laugh. So the same thing exists for us. We have to be real, real careful not to give them more credit than they deserve for either historical perspective or current frame of reference because chances are they're pretty narrow. And then you got to think about, you got to really be sensitive to age. Harder and harder, the older we get. So Tucker and Mike have less problems with it. Bezos got less problems with it than Bill and I and John and Charlie because there's a whole bunch of stuff that is very familiar to us that's still in here that has no connection whatsoever to them. They've either forgotten it, you know, in economics. I'm constantly having to explain to people what the Jimmy Carter economy was because there's a whole bunch of people who were too young, you know, and they're now in, they're running businesses and stuff. There are clients, but they were too young to pay attention. They didn't notice. So they had no frame of reference at all for 22% interest, 22% unemployment, 22% inflation. See, it doesn't exist for them. And if anything you're trying to explain to them is hooked to it, you're kind of in trouble. You know, there's all I've made a little list of things. See, there's like the evening news. See, that's an institution that doesn't exist anymore. So the idea of coming home and sitting down with the TV dinners, that's an antiquated term, right? Very common to some of us in the room, unfamiliar to others in the room. What the hell is that? You know, I said something to somebody the other day about Christmas Club. I got the blank look, you know, right? Because, well, first of all, that was pretty much a product of savings and loans. Well, savings and loans don't even exist. Right? When was the savings and loan crisis? 89. Yes, so Resolute Trust Corporation cleaning up the savings and loan mess. See, McCain was knee deep in that mess, and he doesn't even have to talk about it anymore because there's no particular remembrance of it, knowledge of it, frame of reference for it. So you've got to be very, very careful, either not to be outside of the frame of reference of the people you're writing to, or to give them the frame of reference. And it's natural to assume knowledge on people's part that they don't have, or that they haven't been paying attention to, or that just isn't in their minds anymore. You know, I've often told this story. When I first year of speaking, I was speaking in a, or I was speaking anywhere I could speak. These guys had me in for their real estate awards thing. And I used to just go in office and do a 30 minute presentation, sell something, get out. So it didn't matter what you knew. It didn't make any difference. But besides, they're for like three hours. So they're doing their award ceremony. And they're trotting up. There's a company called 50 States Real Estate. Now I'm standing next to the president of the company in the back of the room. Is they're trotting their people up and giving them their awards, you know. And Susie sold, I don't know, five properties this quarter. And Billy sold seven properties this quarter. And I said, Jesus, did you have anybody doing well? And he kind of looked at me and well, I said, well, they're supposed to be selling real estate, aren't they? So seemingly you'd like sell something once a day. Otherwise, why are you getting out of bed? Well, of course, I came from the direct sales culture where that's, you know, I mean, our little slogan was a sale a day is the M way way. So I have no frame of reference for the fact that you don't sell a property every day. I think that's what you do if you're in sales. You sell something every day. So I said something that, fortunately, I only said it to him, you know, that marked me as a complete moron because I didn't have any frame of reference for their business. Learn to learn to lesson if you're going to go speak to a group. It's a good idea to do a little preparation and get some information ahead of time so you have some understanding of who the hell you're talking to. And a lot of speakers make that some version of that mistake all the time, by the way. So you can't assume that they got it. You got to be real sensitive to what you know is not what they know. We have to deal with what they know has our foundation, has our basis for communicating with them. And, you know, your point, say, the more mature you get in the business you're in, the further away you are from where you were and where they still are. And so it becomes real easy to lapse into lingo and language and industry history that they, you know, they don't know. Okay, let's take our lunch break. Lunch is outside. Do it yourself, deal. Come back in and eat. Let's try and do this in 45 minutes, including bathroom break, smoking break, for those who smoke. So I got 12.15 so that had put us at one o'clock.
